What's going on everybody? Back to sales flow decision making matrix. Let's get jiggy with it. Okay, so now we're on show rates. If you are scheduling a high percentage, the show rate, these are the things that will increase the show rate. Alright, so first off is, are you sending the personal video text? When I say personal video text, I mean personal video text, meaning you have to say their name. It makes an enormous difference whether you say someone's name or not. You have to say, Susan, this is Alex calling from 123, you know, whatever. You have to make it personal and you have to have great energy on there. The people who do the best have great energy on their personal video text. If you look like shit on your personal video text and you don't have energy, like they won't work. Like you have to look like you want that person to actually come genuinely, which you should. But you have to make them believe that. Alright? The next thing is speed to contact. Again, show rates massively improve with speed to contact. Alright, so if you get leads that opt in, you call them, you double dial them, you text them, and then sometimes you can double dial again 20, 30 minutes later, you're going to get them, you'll get in touch with them and then you can get them in same day. If you can get a lead that comes in at four o'clock in by seven o'clock at night, you're going to close them. It just is. Like you can get that much massive action such a short period of time, you're going to close the deal. Alright, and they will show. So which leads me to the next thing, which is always making sure that you're doing same day next day. If you are not doing same day next day, you are sacrificing your show rate and then you just need to go back through the process of overcoming any objections of it being too far away, which is just asking them when they were planning to come to the gym and then saying, cool, let's just do that. And then continually reaffirm, like re-suggesting times that are today and tomorrow. Alright? Beyond that, if you're sure it's low, making sure the reminder sequence is going out. Sometimes there's glitches, just double checking to make sure that your texts are being received. The automated texts, but also that you're actually sending the night before the morning of and the midday reminder text, making sure that you're actually doing that or that the person who is responsible for it is actually doing that. They're not goofing it up or putting wrong names in or anything stupid. Over the phone is important and that's just the energy, your tonality over the phone. Like do you sound like somebody who someone would want to meet with? Do you sound like someone who is in charge, who is going to help this person? Do you sound like a coach or do you sound like an order taker? Right? Like you need to have personality and the person who's making the calls. It is a sale. You're selling the show. You are selling the show. Alright? So that is what you were selling when you were on that phone call. Alright? As a reminder, the scarcity and urgency component, you have to understand that you have to speak as though if that person does not show, their life is not going to change and that is because that is true. They do not show up, their life is not going to change. Everyone's show rates doubled the last week of every campaign because my sales guys knew they were leaving and then all of a sudden they had legitimate scarcity in their voices and then their show rates doubled every single time, which means your conviction and your belief in that will drastically impact how well you do and what your show rates are. Close. Okay, so if you're getting tons of people walking the door and you're not closing, typically the issue is conviction. Like nine times out of ten, it's not any of the other stuff. Everyone wants a one liner, obstacle overcome, they're struggling with blah, blah, blah. Like obstacle overcomes are only really necessary if you fail at the beginning of the sale. Alright? One of the things that I like to say with salespeople are like lawyers. They don't know the answer to, salespeople don't ask for money unless they know they're going to get it. Alright, like if you don't know if you're going to close the person, you need to keep selling before you ask. Alright? Now, conviction. You have to believe, you have to believe that you're going to change this person's life. If you do not believe it, if you do not come in with that intention that like I'm going to change this person's life, don't think I'm going to close this person because it doesn't work that way. You have to think I'm going to help this person, I'm going to help this person make the decision that's going to change their life and I'm going to be the vehicle to help them get there. Alright? If you come from a serving mindset, people will feel it and they will want to work with you. Alright? Conviction is everything. When I work with our sales team, I don't need to drill them on obstacle programs. They know the obstacle programs. They know it because they've already drilled the stuff that you're going to go through in a minute. But it's conviction. That conviction beats skill every day of the week. Alright? Like the people who are the most convicted about their desire to help people and their product and their service close. Okay? The obvious ones with rapport and energy. You have to seem like you're nice. Alright? I check that next. Pain slash need slash gap. Usually people are skipping over the pain portion of the sale and not taking enough time to dig into why they're there and why they failed in the past and why they were not able to succeed on their own. And getting them to admit that. They need to admit fault. They need to admit why they're there and why they haven't been able to succeed and what they have done up to that point and how long it is taking. Alright? So, pain. It is establishing the gap of where they are or where they want to be and they have to own the fact that they are the reason they haven't been successful because they mixed XYZ and that's what we're going to fulfill. And then pre-frame. If you... What are we doing for the pre-frame? Are you... Are they filling out the pre-frame questionnaire when they're walking in the door to get them into the right state for the close? And then also, does your lobby follow the setup that I've already recommended with the testimonial wall with the certifications, with stack of contracts, with the way the seats are positioned. All of those things matter because you wouldn't like... If you can make an extra 10% close rate on an entire campaign, then you might make an extra five grand or an extra 10 grand. It's worth taking the couple hours to do this. Alright? Then finally, back out. Do the integrity tie down. Typically, back outs aren't a huge issue but I'm just covering it just in case they are. Do the integrity tie down which is at the end of this training. And I don't know what I mean by get at least 100 today. Oh, get at least $100 today. Yeah, make sure that you're always getting at least $100 down on the day. That's going to decrease your back outs. The more you get down on the down payment, the less the person is to back out. So if you're having lots of like $50, weird partial pay, weird payment schedules, usually you just need to sell harder, people have money. They're just choosing not to give to you because they don't value what you're saying. And then finally, too many delay closes which is kind of piggyback on the $100 down which is if you're having way too many people who are like, yeah, I'll put my first payment for next Friday, things like that. It means you're not selling hard enough and you're not really convincing them. So you need to go back through the sales training. And just as one final piece here, from an overarching mindset standpoint when you're looking at your sales, there are some people who enter programs with the intention to prove to themselves that they are a victim, that nothing can work because of their XYZ, because of their circumstances, because of their environment, because of their whatever. And then there are some people who choose to say, I will succeed despite my circumstances, despite my upbringing, despite the area, despite the whatever, despite my whatever it is, insert excuse here. All right. And so some people try just enough to say that they tried and there are other people who play to win. Like, it's just a question of who you want to be. So I leave you with the question, how hard are you trying? Just as a note and something that has become very common in our community, these sales trainings that you're about to enter and watch, many people watch them on a daily basis during the day, to watch. So they watch them on a daily basis on repeat. Why? Because it takes repetition to learn a skill. You're not going to watch them once and learn it because you want to start programming your subconscious mind. You want to be unconscious, you want to have unconscious competence. You want to be so good that you don't have to think about it. Right? You go from unconscious and competence, which is like, you don't know how much you suck. Conscious and competence, which is, you know that you suck. Conscious competence, you have to try and you can be good, but you have to really like think about what you're saying, think about the sequence, and then unconscious competence, you can do the stuff in your sleep. That's what you want to get to. And the only way to get there is through repetition. And the question is, do you want to repeat yourself with prospects that you're paying for and fail in front of them? Or do you want to repeat it in the privacy of your mind when you were listening to these so that you can get some of these bad reps out of the way so that when you are in the game, you've already done enough practice to win? Okay? So I'm going to say it again, repeat. Do them more than once. Total volume is going to matter. And the question is, how hard are you willing to try? And really like, do you want to win?
